planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; scarlet kadsura is dioecious — plant male and female plants for fruit production. It grows naturally in shaded woodland understory conditions and tolerates full shade.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade in zones 7–10; scarlet kadsura is a semi-evergreen to evergreen twining vine native to Japan, Korea, and southern China, valued for exceptional seasonal interest across multiple seasons: glossy, dark green, leathery leaves that may turn burgundy in cool weather; small, pale yellow, fragrant flowers in summer; and spectacular, showy clusters of scarlet-red, coral-like fruiting bodies in fall — each cluster up to 2 inches in diameter and resembling a bunch of tiny rubies. The fruits are edible and used in traditional Chinese medicine. It grows naturally in shaded forest understories and tolerates deep shade better than most fruiting vines — one of its most valuable garden traits. An outstanding shade vine for zone 7–10 gardens where its combination of handsome evergreen foliage, fragrant flowers, and dramatic red fruit clusters provides year-round ornamental interest in difficult, shaded positions.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; bay starvine grows naturally in moist, rich, shaded woodland understory conditions in the southern Appalachians and adjacent areas and is dioecious — male and female plants are required for fruit set.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade at forest edges and woodland understory positions; bay starvine is the native North American member of the Schisandraceae — the same family as the famous five-flavor berry (S. chinensis) of Chinese medicine. It grows in the moist, shaded ravines, stream bottoms, and rich forest understory of the southern Appalachians and adjacent lowlands, producing small red flowers in spring that require cross-pollination between male and female plants. The fruits, clustered like a miniature bunch of grapes on an elongated stalk, ripen red in late summer. The plant tolerates deep shade better than most fruiting vines, filling a valuable niche in shaded woodland garden designs. Though less medicinally celebrated than the Asian species, the native bay starvine has similar phytochemical constituents. A rare and beautiful native vine deserving wider use in woodland shade gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in fertile, moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; threeleaf akebia is slightly more vigorous than fiveleaf akebia (A. quinata) and produces larger fruits. Cross-pollinate with plants from different sources for best fruit set.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in zones 5–9; threeleaf akebia is closely related to the more commonly grown fiveleaf akebia (A. quinata) but is distinguished by having only three leaflets per leaf (vs. five in A. quinata) and generally larger fruits — the sausage-shaped, blue-purple pods can reach 4–5 inches long in good specimens. The pale purple, vanilla-fragrant flowers in spring are striking and ornamental. The ripe fruits split open along one side to reveal white, jelly-like pulp surrounding black seeds — the pulp is eaten fresh, tasting sweet and somewhat like lychee, while the empty pods are used as a vegetable in Japan. A vigorous, somewhat twining vine that can be invasive in some regions; grow on a sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence where its vigor can be managed by pruning. A productive edible vine for the partial shade garden in zones 5–9.

planting.note: Carefully transplant small rooted sections with attached soil and intact root structure in early spring; spotted wintergreen requires very specific conditions — dry, infertile, sandy or rocky, strongly acidic soil in deep shade with established ectomycorrhizal fungi. Only plant nursery-propagated stock; never collect from the wild.

directions: Plant in dry, lean, sandy or rocky, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in deep shade; spotted wintergreen is one of the most beautiful native evergreen groundcovers of the eastern forest understory — a low, spreading plant with glossy, dark green leaves dramatically patterned with white or pale silver along the veins, creating a vivid variegated effect. Small, waxy, fragrant white flowers are produced in summer on upright stalks. The plant requires the presence of specific ectomycorrhizal fungi and strongly dislikes root disturbance, fertilization, or soil amendments — making it notoriously difficult to establish in cultivated settings. The best approach is to acquire nursery-propagated plugs (never wild-collected), plant in dry, acidic, sandy soil near the base of oaks or pines that support appropriate mycorrhizal networks, and then largely leave it undisturbed. Once established, it spreads slowly to form colonies that are permanent features of the native shade garden.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in average to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; grape honeysuckle grows naturally in moist woodland edges and thickets, tolerating a range of soil conditions. It is a non-invasive native alternative to Japanese honeysuckle.

directions: Plant in average to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun at woodland edges and on fences, trellises, and arbors; grape honeysuckle is a native Midwest and northeastern vine with a unique feature: the paired leaves immediately below the flowers are fused at their bases into a single disk-like structure through which the stem appears to grow — a characteristic called 'perfoliate' leaves that immediately distinguishes it from other honeysuckles. Tubular, pale yellow to cream flowers in late spring attract hummingbirds and long-tongued bumblebees. Red berries follow in summer and fall, providing food for numerous birds. A non-invasive native alternative to the notorious invasive Japanese honeysuckle (L. japonica) that can be used on trellises, fences, and into shrub borders without concerns about spreading. Tolerates partial shade better than many flowering vines.
